title: Detecting Circular References in a Linked List

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.

examples.explanation: The given linked list has no circular references.
The given linked list has a circular reference.
The given linked list has no circular references.

hints.text: You need to traverse the entire linked list to detect whether it is circular or not.
If you find a node again during traversal, the linked list has a circular reference.
Consider edge cases such as an empty linked list or lists with one node with no circular references.

editorial.title: Detecting Circular References in a Linked List
editorial.intuition: The task is similar to detecting a cycle in a linked list using Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm but with additional edge cases.
editorial.optimalSolution: You can use a boolean array of size equal to the size of the linked list to track visited nodes.

starterCode.java:
public class Solution {
    public boolean hasCircularReference(int[] head);
starterCode.python:
class Solution:
    def hasCircularReference(self, head: List[int]) -> bool:

solutions.python:
import sys
sys.stdin.readline
n = int(sys.stdin.readline())
head = list(map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split()))
# test code

def hasCircularReference(head):
    visited = [False] * n
    i = 0
    while i < n:
        if visited[i]:
            return True
        visited[i] = True
        i = head[i]
    return False

if not hasCircularReference(head):
    sys.stdout.write('False')
else:
    sys.stdout.write('True')
